original_abstract:
This paper introduces MCTest, a new open-domain dataset specifically designed to challenge and evaluate machine comprehension of text. While previous efforts in semantic modeling have focused on restricted domains or specific tasks like relation extraction, MCTest requires systems to answer multiple-choice questions based on elementary-level fictional stories. This format offers a clear, objective metric while testing sophisticated linguistic abilities, such as causal reasoning and world knowledge. Because the stories are fictional, the task encourages deep internal processing of the text rather than simple retrieval from external knowledge bases like Wikipedia.

The authors detail a highly scalable and cost-effective crowdsourcing pipeline for data collection. To ensure high quality without the expense of manual editing, they implement a multi-stage validation process involving worker pre-screening with grammar tests and subsequent peer rating. The resulting dataset consists of 500 stories and 2,000 questions, curated to match the quality of hand-edited sets at a fraction of the cost. Performance baselines using lexical sliding-window and distance-based algorithms, as well as recognizing textual entailment (RTE) systems, demonstrate that the task remains significantly challenging for current state-of-the-art methods. MCTest is offered as a free resource to foster community-wide progress toward the high-level goal of open-domain machine reading.
humanized_abstract:
We introduce a new open-domain evaluation set for assessing machine comprehension of text, called MCTest. The semantic modeling and evaluation communities have addressed a few different challenges, including: (1) limited domain (e.g., Freebase, MMIC, Wikidata and WordNet); (2) limited scope (e.g., relation extraction and question answering tasks); and (3) limited amount of training and evaluation data (e.g., Basic Questions and QAngaroo). Our goal is to create a new, more open-ended challenge for evaluation of semantic understanding. Our MCTest dataset is a set of elementary grade fictional stories with which a program must be able to engage in a game of multiple choice question and answer. Although the evaluation metric of the task is straightforward to define, the skills required to win the game are correspondingly complex, as they include deep comprehension of a narrative, including the ability to infer causal relationships, and to understand phenomena that are only implied by the text. Because the stories are fictional, the model must actually understand what is written, rather than simply being able to look up answers in a knowledge base.

We describe a scalable and low-cost crowdsourcing workflow for data collection. To avoid the expense of manual proofreading, we employ a validation process that includes grammar tests for pre-screening workers and peer review of produced content. Our dataset, which consists of 500 stories with 2,000 questions, achieves similar quality to manually proofread datasets at a fraction of the cost. We report performance benchmarks for typical approaches to this task, including lexical sliding-window and distance-based algorithms, as well as a couple of Textual Entailment (RTE) systems. We release MCTest as an open, community-developed benchmark for advancing the field of open-domain machine reading. The full dataset is freely available for research use.

document.input:
This paper introduces MCTest, a new open-domain dataset specifically designed to challenge and evaluate machine comprehension of text. While previous efforts in semantic modeling have focused on restricted domains or specific tasks like relation extraction, MCTest requires systems to answer multiple-choice questions based on elementary-level fictional stories. This format offers a clear, objective metric while testing sophisticated linguistic abilities, such as causal reasoning and world knowledge. Because the stories are fictional, the task encourages deep internal processing of the text rather than simple retrieval from external knowledge bases like Wikipedia.

The authors detail a highly scalable and cost-effective crowdsourcing pipeline for data collection. To ensure high quality without the expense of manual editing, they implement a multi-stage validation process involving worker pre-screening with grammar tests and subsequent peer rating. The resulting dataset consists of 500 stories and 2,000 questions, curated to match the quality of hand-edited sets at a fraction of the cost. Performance baselines using lexical sliding-window and distance-based algorithms, as well as recognizing textual entailment (RTE) systems, demonstrate that the task remains significantly challenging for current state-of-the-art methods. MCTest is offered as a free resource to foster community-wide progress toward the high-level goal of open-domain machine reading.
